title: Dog Stiffness Getting Up in the Morning: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog stiff when waking up? Learn about morning stiffness, why it's usually low urgency, and how video triage helps your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Morning stiffness in dogs is typically a sign of chronic joint issues like arthritis and usually requires a scheduled vet visit rather than emergency care. Providing your vet with a video of your dog waking up at home is one of the most effective ways to ensure an accurate diagnosis.

What exactly is morning stiffness in dogs and why does it happen?

Morning stiffness, often referred to as 'startup stiffness,' is a common condition where a dog appears slow, rigid, or hesitant to move immediately after waking up. In many cases, the dog's gait improves after they have walked around for a few minutes and their joints have 'warmed up.' This is frequently a clinical sign of osteoarthritis, age-related joint degeneration, or soft tissue inflammation.

Is my dog's morning stiffness considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for morning stiffness is typically low, as it is usually a chronic, progressive issue rather than a sudden crisis.
  • Schedule a non-emergency appointment with your veterinarian to discuss long-term management and comfort.
  • Seek urgent veterinary care if your dog is completely unable to stand, is yelping in pain, or has suddenly lost coordination.

Why should I record a video of my dog's stiffness for the veterinarian?

  • Adrenaline at the vet's office often masks symptoms, so a video of your dog at home is a vital diagnostic tool.
  • Capture a 30-second clip of your dog's transition from sleep to movement to show which limbs are most affected.
  • Visual evidence allows the vet to see the degree of lameness in a natural, relaxed setting.
  • Videos help your veterinarian track the effectiveness of treatments and monitor the progression of the condition over time.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Morning stiffness in dogs can be attributed to various causes, including idiopathic polyarthritis (common in large breeds like German Shepherds and retrievers), joint trauma such as cranial cruciate ligament rupture, and general arthritis resulting in pain and altered joint function. Clinical signs of arthritis include lameness, especially after rest, and pain upon joint manipulation. Diagnosis involves radiography to assess joint damage and arthroscopy for accurate evaluation of articular cartilage. Treatment for acute synovitis and capsulitis includes rest, physical therapy (cold water treatment, ice, passive flexion, and swimming), and NSAIDs such as phenylbutazone. In more severe cases, joint lavage may be necessary.

Chapter: Orthopedics, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1198)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin for their stiffness?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give human pain relievers to dogs without specific veterinary instruction, as many are toxic. Your vet can prescribe pet-safe anti-inflammatories.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to morning stiffness?
faqs.answer: Yes, large breeds like German Shepherds, Labradors, and Golden Retrievers are more predisposed to joint issues, though it can affect any dog as they age.

faqs.question: Does a heated bed help with dog joint stiffness?
faqs.answer: Yes, orthopedic beds and gentle warmth can help soothe aching joints and may make it easier for your dog to transition from rest to movement.
